questions.scenario: The app cannot read a secret during release. A teammate wants to grant Owner for five minutes. What is the best response?

options.text: Use the error to identify the missing permission and grant the smallest role at the smallest useful scope, then verify.
Grant Owner because temporary broad access leaves no risk.
Disable audit logs so the release can move faster.
Move the secret into Git so Secret Manager no longer blocks the app.

questions.explanation: A narrow fix follows the access sentence. Temporary broad access can still create real exposure and often becomes permanent.

questions.scenario: IAM policy looks correct, but access still fails. The binding has a condition. What should the team do?

options.text: Check whether the condition matches the request before removing or widening access.
Delete every condition because conditions are always mistakes.
Change the billing account because conditions only apply to invoices.
Assume the principal is wrong without checking evidence.

questions.explanation: Conditions can narrow when a binding applies. They are part of the access relationship and should be understood before changing them.

questions.scenario: Which evidence helps verify a narrow IAM fix?

options.text: Error or audit evidence showing the resource and permission.
A screenshot of an unrelated project where access works.

questions.explanation: Verification should prove the intended principal can perform the intended action on the intended resource. Unrelated project evidence does not prove the production fix.
